ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a glutinous rice ball parent class but no specific entry for tang yuan served in cold soy bean milk. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for tang yuan in soy milk. A0CKP (rice-based sweet snack) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for tang yuan or glutinous rice ball dessert in soy milk.

serving.description: 1 family sharing bowl (10 rice balls with soy bean milk)
serving.natural_language_portion: One family sharing bowl contains approximately 10 glutinous rice balls (each approximately 30–35 g) served in cold soy bean milk, totalling approximately 600 g per bowl.

nutrient_source.title: Tang Yuan (Glutinous Rice Balls) with Soy Milk — composite estimation from HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (glutinous rice flour, soy milk, sugar, peanut/sesame/red bean fillings)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo) and USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB Foodo entry exists for this exact product (10 tang yuan in cold soy bean milk, family bowl). Nutrient values are estimated from HPB Foodo entries for tang yuan (plain and filled) and soy bean milk, weighted by approximate composition: ~55% glutinous rice balls (mixed fillings: peanut, sesame, red bean) and ~45% soy bean milk by weight. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy used for cross-validation of glutinous rice flour and soy milk macros. Values represent per-100 g of the combined dish as served.

gi_source.title: Glutinous rice ball (tang yuan) — GI analogous estimate based on glutinous rice products; soy milk GI reference from University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tang yuan in cold soy bean milk. GI estimated at 55 based on: (1) glutinous rice products in the University of Sydney GI Database typically range GI 65–98 for plain glutinous rice; however, tang yuan as small filled balls in liquid have a lower effective GI due to fat and protein from fillings (peanut, sesame) and soy milk matrix, which slow gastric emptying. (2) Soy milk GI is approximately 30–44 per University of Sydney GI Database. (3) Weighted composite GI estimated at approximately 55 (low), reflecting the moderating effect of soy protein, fat from fillings, and the cold serving temperature. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 141 g carbohydrate in 600 g serving) / 100 ≈ 78.5. Carbohydrate per serving = 23.5 g/100g × 600 g = 141 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~38 mg/100 g); soy isoflavones may have modest blood-pressure-lowering effects
hypertension.guidance: Tang yuan in soy bean milk is low in sodium (~228 mg per 600 g serving) and is generally suitable for individuals with hypertension when consumed as an occasional dessert, though the moderate sugar content should be noted.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High carbohydrate load per serving (~141 g per family bowl) and moderate-to-high glycemic index of glutinous rice component
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise caution with this dish: a single family sharing bowl provides approximately 141 g of carbohydrate, which significantly exceeds a single-meal carbohydrate target; portion control is strongly advised and the dish should not be consumed without accounting for its glycemic impact within the daily carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat content from peanut and sesame fillings; soy protein may confer modest cardioprotective benefit; cholesterol-free
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tang yuan in soy bean milk is cholesterol-free and contains predominantly unsaturated fats from peanut and sesame fillings, which are not adversely associated with cardiovascular risk; however, the high carbohydrate and sugar content of a full family bowl warrants portion moderation for individuals managing cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Glutinous Rice Ball in Soy Bean Milk (Cold) — Family Sharing Bowl, known in Chinese as 十全十美汤圆豆浆 (Shí Quán Shí Měi Tāng Yuán Dòu Jiāng), is a traditional Chinese dessert dish served in Singapore, particularly during the Dong Zhi (Winter Solstice) festival. The dish consists of ten glutinous rice balls (tang yuan, 汤圆) served in cold, lightly sweetened soy bean milk. The name 十全十美 means 'perfect in every way' or 'ten completions of beauty', with the number ten symbolising completeness and wholeness in Chinese culture. Tang yuan are made from glutinous rice flour dough shaped into smooth, chewy spheres, typically filled with sweet pastes such as ground peanut, black sesame, or red bean. The balls are boiled until they float, then chilled and served in cold soy bean milk, creating a refreshing contrast to the traditional hot version. The texture is soft, chewy, and slightly sticky, with a sweet, nutty filling that contrasts with the mild, creamy soy milk base. Nutritionally, this dish is moderately energy-dense at approximately 148 kcal per 100 g, with carbohydrate as the dominant macronutrient (23.5 g/100 g), moderate protein from soy milk (3.8 g/100 g), and moderate fat from nut-based fillings (4.2 g/100 g). The dish is cholesterol-free, dairy-free, and suitable for vegetarians and vegans. Clinically, the high carbohydrate load of a full family bowl (~141 g carbohydrate per 600 g serving) makes this a high-glycemic-load meal that requires careful portion management for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The soy bean milk base contributes isoflavones and plant protein, which may offer modest cardiovascular and bone health benefits. Sodium content is low, making it broadly suitable for individuals managing hypertension when consumed in moderation.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact product (10 tang yuan in cold soy bean milk, family sharing bowl). Nutrient values are estimated from HPB Singapore Foodo tang yuan and soy bean milk entries, cross-validated with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for glutinous rice products and soy milk, with a moderating adjustment for fat/protein content of fillings. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component foods are well-characterised in approved sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are per 100 g of the combined dish as served (rice balls + soy bean milk in approximately 55:45 ratio by weight). 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this dish contains no hydrogenated fats; all fats are from natural plant sources (peanut, sesame, soy). 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as all ingredients are plant-based. 4. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; glutinous rice flour and soy bean milk contain negligible vitamin C. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; no vitamin D-fortified soy milk is assumed in this restaurant preparation. 7. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy entries for these component foods at the level of precision required. 8. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary HPB Foodo source for tang yuan. 9. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the primary HPB Foodo source for this dish. 10. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary HPB Foodo source for this dish. 11. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy entries for these components. 12. GI is set to 55 (low) as an analogous estimate; direct GI measurement for this specific product does not exist in the University of Sydney GI Database. 13. Glycemic load per serving (78.5) is high due to the large serving size (600 g) and high carbohydrate content; this is clinically significant for diabetes management. 14. The food_group has been corrected from the input value 'Beverages' to 'Desserts', as tang yuan in soy bean milk is a dessert dish, not a beverage. 15. Serving size of 600 g is estimated for a family sharing bowl of 10 rice balls with soy bean milk, based on typical restaurant portion sizes for this format.
